perturbation.original_text: E) to take all proper and reasonable measures to ensure the confidentiality and security of the Confidential Information at all times.
perturbation.changed_text: E) to take measures they deem commercially reasonable to ensure the confidentiality and security of the Confidential Information at all times.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'all proper and reasonable measures' with 'measures they deem commercially reasonable' introduces subjectivity and weakens the obligation. The phrase 'commercially reasonable' is open to interpretation and could allow the recipient to prioritize business interests over security, even if such measures are insufficient.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 32 of the GDPR requires implementation of 'appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk'. Allowing the recipient to take only 'commercially reasonable' measures may fall short of the GDPR's requirement for 'appropriate' measures, especially if commercially reasonable measures are insufficient to protect personal data involved in the confidential information. The contract applies to England and Wales, where GDPR has been incorporated into national law.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text introduces ambiguity that weakens the original obligation to ensure confidentiality and security. By replacing 'all proper and reasonable measures' with 'measures they deem commercially reasonable,' the changed text allows the recipient to prioritize business interests over data protection, which may not meet the GDPR's standard of 'appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk' as discussed in the Law Explanation. The scraped snippet alludes to rules for businesses and organizations and mentions 'obligations' to comply with EU data protection rules. The changed text makes the recipient's obligation subjective which creates a legal contradiction by potentially failing to uphold GDPR requirements.

perturbation.original_text: G) to destroy immediately on our written request all Confidential Information in your possession including any copies made (save where we are required pursuant to some law or enactment or regulation or the rules of any Stock Exchange to retain originals or copies of any of the Confidential Information).
perturbation.changed_text: G) to make good faith efforts to destroy on our written request all Confidential Information in your possession including any copies made (save where we are required pursuant to some law or enactment or regulation or the rules of any Stock Exchange to retain originals or copies of any of the Confidential Information).
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'to destroy immediately' with 'to make good faith efforts to destroy' significantly weakens the obligation. 'Good faith efforts' is a vague term that doesn't guarantee complete destruction and opens the door to disputes about whether sufficient effort was made. The immediate destruction clause is important for protecting commercially sensitive information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Regulation 3 outlines measures, procedures and remedies that are made available if a trade secret is unlawfully acquired, used or disclosed. The original clause makes it clear that the recipient must destroy the confidential information immediately upon request to safeguard the trade secret. This strengthens the owner's position in the event of misuse. By changing the clause to 'make good faith efforts to destroy', it weakens the protection of the trade secret as it does not guarantee destruction of trade secrets when requested. This creates ambiguity and could be seen as an inadequate measure to protect the trade secret, potentially violating the Trade Secrets Regulations.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates immediate destruction of confidential information upon written request, whereas the changed text requires only 'good faith efforts' to destroy such information. The Law Explanation states that weakening the clause to 'make good faith efforts' weakens the protection of the trade secret and does not guarantee destruction of trade secrets when requested. This creates ambiguity and could be seen as an inadequate measure to protect the trade secret, potentially violating the Trade Secrets Regulations. The modified text introduces ambiguity where there was previously a clear obligation, creating a legal contradiction as described in the Perturbation Definitions.

perturbation.original_text: 3) that this agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the law of England and Wales and the courts of England and Wales shall have exclusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute or claim that arises out of or in connection with it or its subject matter or formation (including non-contractual disputes or claims)
perturbation.changed_text: 3) that this agreement shall be generally interpreted under the guiding principles of the law of England and Wales, and disputes may be resolved in the courts of England and Wales.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the law of England and Wales and the courts of England and Wales shall have exclusive jurisdiction' to 'shall be generally interpreted under the guiding principles of the law of England and Wales, and disputes may be resolved in the courts of England and Wales' removes the certainty of exclusive jurisdiction and replaces it with a more discretionary approach. This creates ambiguity about where disputes must be resolved, potentially leading to forum shopping and increased litigation costs. The words 'guiding principles' are also quite vague.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Hague Convention on Choice of Court Agreements
perturbation.law_explanation: Although the UK isn't technically bound by the Hague Convention on Choice of Court Agreements anymore (having denounced it post-Brexit), the principle of honoring exclusive jurisdiction clauses remains a strong aspect of international commercial law. The modified text, by removing the explicit statement of 'exclusive jurisdiction,' could be interpreted as a non-exclusive jurisdiction clause. A non-exclusive jurisdiction clause would mean a court outside of England and Wales may take up a case under certain circumstances. This can lead to a violation of recognizing and enforcing choice of court agreements as understood by international law and conventions such as the Hague convention.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Article III | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Article III Primary tabs Section 1. The judicial power of the United States, shall be vested in one Supreme Court, and in such inferior courts as the Congress may from time to time ordain and establish. The judges, both of the supreme and inferior courts, shall hold their offices during good behaviour, and shall, at stated times, receive for their services, a compensation, which shall not be diminished during their continuance in office. Section 2. The judicial power shall extend to all cases, in law and equity, arising under this Constitution, the laws of the United States, and treaties made, or which shall be made, under their authority;--to all cases affecting ambassadors, other public ministers and consuls;--to all cases of admiralty and maritime jurisdiction;--to controversies to which the United States shall be a party;--to controversies between two or more states;-- between a state and citizens of another state ;--between citizens of different states;--between citizens of the same state claiming lands under grants of different states, and between a state, or the citizens thereof, and foreign states, citizens or subjects. In all cases affecting ambassadors, other public ministers and consuls, and those in which a state shall be party, the Supreme Court shall have original jurisdiction. In all the other cases before mentioned, the Supreme Court shall have appellate jurisdiction, both as to law and fact, with such exceptions, and under such regulations as the Congress shall make. The trial of all crimes, except in cases of impeachment, shall be by jury; and such trial shall be held in the state where the said crimes shall have been committed; but when not committed within any state, the trial shall be at such place or places as the Congress may by law have directed. Section 3. Treason against the United States, shall consist only in levying war against them, or in adhering to their enemies, giving them aid and comfort. No person shall be convicted of treason unless on the testimony of two witnesses to the same overt act, or on confession in open court. The Congress shall have power to declare the punishment of treason, but no attainder of treason shall work corruption of blood, or forfeiture except during the life of the person attainted. ‹ Article II up Article IV › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Service
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: ee also defines treason . Section 1 of Article Three vests the judicial power of the United States in "one supreme Court", as well as "inferior courts" established by Congress. Section 1 authorizes the creation of inferior courts, but does not require it; the first inferior federal courts were established shortly after the ratification of the Constitution with the Judiciary Act of 1789 . Section 1 also establishes that federal judges do not face term limits, and that an individual judge's salary may not be decreased. Article Three does not set the size of the Supreme Court or establish specific positions on the court, but Article One establishes the position of chief justice . Along with the Vesting Clauses of Article One and Article Two , Article Three's Vesting Clause establishes the separation of powers among the three branches of government. Section 2 of Article Three delineates federal judicial power. The Case or Controversy Clause restricts the judiciary's power to actual cases and controversies, meaning that federal judicial power does not extend to cases which are hypothetical, or which are proscribed due to standing , mootness , or ripeness issues. Section 2 states that the federal judiciary's power extends to cases arising under the Constitution, federal laws, federal treaties, controversies involving multiple states or foreign powers, and other enumerated areas. Section 2 gives the Supreme Court original jurisdiction when ambassadors, public officials, or the states are a party in the case, leaving the Supreme Court with appellate jurisdiction in all other areas to which the federal judiciary's jurisdiction extends. Section 2 also gives Congress the power to strip the Supreme Court of appellate jurisdiction, and establishes that all federal crimes must be tried before a jury . Section 2 does not expressly grant the federal judiciary the power of judicial review , but the courts have exercised this power since the 1803 case of Marbury v. Madison . Section 3 of Article Three defines treason and empowers Congress to punish treason. Section 3 requires that at least two witnesses testify to the treasonous act, or that the individual accused of treason confess in open court. It also limits the ways in which Congress can punish those convicted of treason. Background [ edit ] Unlike the Articles of Confederation , the US Constitution separated the legislative, executive and judicial powers. Article III separates and places the judicial power in the judiciary. This idea is most often attributed to Montesquieu . Although not the progenitor, Montesquieu's writing on the separation of power in The Spirit of Laws was immensely influential on the U.S. Constitution. [ 1 ] Section 1: Federal courts [ edit ] Section 1 is one of the three vesting clauses of the United States Constitution , which vests the judicial power of the United States in federal courts, requires the supreme court, allows inferior courts, requires good behavior tenure for judges, and prohibits decreasing the salaries of judges. The judicial Power of the United States, shall be vested in one supreme Court, and in such inferior Courts as the Congress may from time to time ordain and establish. The Judges, both of the supreme and inferior Courts, shall hold their Offices during good Behaviour, and shall, at stated Times, receive for their Services a Compensation which shall not be diminished during their Continuance in Office. The Committee of Detail report reads slightly differently: [ 2 ] [ 3 ] The Judicial Power of the United States shall be vested in one supreme Court, and in such Inferior Courts as shall, when necessary, from time to time, be constituted by the Legislature of the United States. Clause 1: Vesting of judicial power and number of courts [ edit ] Main article: Judicial Vesting Clause Article III authorizes one Supreme Court, but does not set the number of justices that must be appointed to it. perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall be governed by... exclusive jurisdiction' to 'generally interpreted under... disputes may be resolved' significantly weakens the binding nature of the jurisdiction clause. This creates ambiguity, which contradicts the principle of honoring exclusive jurisdiction clauses. While the scraped snippets from the U.S. Constitution (Article III) don't directly address international jurisdiction clauses, the LLM-generated law explanation highlights that removing 'exclusive jurisdiction' can lead to a non-exclusive interpretation, potentially violating the recognition and enforcement of choice of court agreements.
